# Kudu YC S22 Rejection Email: Slack Alternative Market Concern

- Type: External founder blog post; YC rejection email excerpt
- Status: External public source; user-verified rejection email excerpt
- URL: https://liorn.substack.com/p/rejected-from-ycombinator
- Author: Lior Neu-ner
- Published: 2022-06-27
- Captured: 2026-06-16
- Method: source page fetched; pasted excerpt checked against the page
- Caveat: references/caveats/personalized-yc-rejection-email.md

## Summary

- Lior Neu-ner says he applied to YC S22 for Kudu, a Slack alternative, received two YC interviews, and was rejected after the second interview.
- The rejection email's central concern was not founder ability. It worried that Slack was deeply embedded, making it hard for users to pay attention to a new tool, and that incremental product improvements might not be enough to win customers and scale.
- Use this as an external example when reviewing applications that compete with deeply embedded incumbents: pressure-test the wedge, urgency, first-customer path, switching behavior, and whether the product is more than an incremental improvement.
- Do not treat this as a YC-wide rejection template, current YC policy, or a reason to predict another company's outcome. It is one public founder-posted rejection email.

## Content

### Article Context

Lior Neu-ner writes that he was building a Slack alternative, applied to Y Combinator's S22 batch, had two interviews, and was rejected after the second interview. He says a large part of the second interview focused on how he would acquire customers, and that he did not have a good answer.

### Rejection Email

Hi Lior,

Unfortunately, we've decided not to fund Kudu this batch. We appreciate you coming back to interview again and we enjoyed talking with you both times. You're clearly a talented engineer and building with a deep passion for improving on Slack.

However we worry that Slack is now so deeply embedded into every company, it’s incredibly hard to get users to pay attention to a new tool. We’ve no doubt it will happen eventually but we suspect it will require a product that creates a completely new paradigm and no one saw coming. Similar to how Facebook eventually became the biggest threat to Google’s ad business. So while we think you have a great set of ideas to make incremental improvements to Slack's product, we worry that won't be enough for you to get customers and scale this.

We know we’re often wrong when trying to predict the future like this and we’d love to hear from you again in the future as you continue building the company. Thanks for taking the time to interview with us.
